text: The GOAL-SPECIFIC router a-0026 named WORKS within q-0011's retrieval proxy but only partially realizes the concentrated-need advantage. e-0026 found cross-domain need is concentrated (59.5% of cross-domain goals need exactly one foreign cluster) yet the static top-K bridge router (same K neighbours for every goal in a home) caps complete coverage at 0.674 (K=5). e-0030 drops the static base entirely and routes home + the top-m foreign clusters PREDICTED from the goal's own NAME tokens (token x foreign-cluster co-occurrence P(d|t)=n(t,d)/n(t) weighted by IDF, train-only), comparing head-to-head at matched complete-coverage on 432 held-out cross-domain TEST theorems. The goal router beats the static one: higher ceiling at matched breadth (b=5: 0.729 vs 0.674; b=10: 0.914 vs 0.859) and reaches the 0.70-0.91 band at 17-26% fewer candidate decls (0.674: goal 53.9k vs static 72.8k, 0.74x; 0.90: goal 72.3k vs static 87.4k, 0.83x). But the realizable name-token predictor leaves large headroom: an ORACLE goal router reaches 0.93 coverage at just ~21.8k candidates (24% of the library) vs the predictor's ~72k -- a ~4x potential cut. The predictor has a HUB-BIAS: at matched breadth it routes large token-associated clusters (P(d|t) favours globally-popular foreign clusters) rather than the small specialized clusters a cross-domain goal actually needs. Net for q-0011: a per-goal name-token router is the right structural lever and beats the static router, but a less hub-biased / content-aware target predictor is the remaining lever to realize the cheap near-exhaustive routing the oracle shows is possible. Still a premise-availability upper bound, not a measured prover success rate or transfer.
